---
title: "Lectures 5--6 — Linear Systems and Iterative Methods"
subtitle: "Handout: Foundations of and Exercises in Numerical Analysis"
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-title: "Contents"
    number-sections: true
    html-math-method: mathjax
    theme: cosmo
    code-fold: false
    code-tools: true
    highlight-style: github
    # Experimental v4 annotation overlay: stores stroke points as
    # anchor-relative fractional coordinates so they follow browser
    # page-zoom (and notes scale with the surrounding text).
    include-after-body:
      - ../annotation-v4.html
execute:
  echo: true
  eval: true
jupyter: python3
---

<script>
window.__ANNOTATION_V4_AUTOLOAD__ = {
  url: "lectures-5-6-linear-systems-and-iterative-methods__2026-05-22-08-22-16.json",
  enable: true,
  tool: "cursor",
};
</script>

<style>
/* Slide-like styling only for the introductory part of this handout. */
#sec-linear-systems,
#sec-direct-iterative {
  margin: 2.2rem 0;
}

#sec-linear-systems > h2,
#sec-direct-iterative > h2 {
  margin: 0 0 1rem;
  padding: 0.75rem 1rem;
  border-left: 8px solid #3b6cff;
  border-radius: 12px;
  background: #eef4ff;
  color: #17356f;
  font-size: 1.9rem;
  font-weight: 800;
}

.intro-slide {
  margin: 1.25rem 0;
  padding: 1.45rem 1.6rem;
  border: 1px solid #d9e3f7;
  border-radius: 20px;
  background: linear-gradient(135deg, #ffffff 0%, #f6f9ff 100%);
  box-shadow: 0 12px 28px rgba(31, 45, 61, 0.10);
}

.intro-slide h3 {
  margin-top: 0;
  color: #244fa3;
  font-weight: 800;
  letter-spacing: 0.01em;
}

.intro-slide strong {
  color: #d9480f;
  font-weight: 800;
}

.big-message {
  margin: 0.7rem 0 1rem;
  color: #17233c;
  font-size: 1.35rem;
  font-weight: 750;
  line-height: 1.45;
}

.hero-equation {
  margin: 1rem auto;
  padding: 0.75rem 1rem;
  max-width: 360px;
  border: 1px solid #c8d6f5;
  border-radius: 14px;
  background: #f1f5ff;
  color: #17356f;
  text-align: center;
  box-shadow: 0 4px 10px rgba(31, 45, 61, 0.06);
}

.hero-equation mjx-container {
  color: #17356f;
  font-size: 125%;
}

.symbol-strip,
.tag-row {
  display: flex;
  flex-wrap: wrap;
  gap: 0.55rem;
  margin-top: 0.8rem;
}

.tag {
  display: inline-block;
  padding: 0.35rem 0.65rem;
  border-radius: 999px;
  background: #e9f0ff;
  color: #284b9b;
  font-weight: 700;
}

.slide-grid,
.method-grid {
  display: grid;
  gap: 1rem;
  grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fit, minmax(220px, 1fr));
}

.slide-card,
.method-card {
  padding: 1rem 1.05rem;
  border-radius: 16px;
  background: #ffffff;
  border: 1px solid #e2e8f0;
  box-shadow: 0 6px 16px rgba(31, 45, 61, 0.07);
}

.slide-card h4,
.method-card h3 {
  margin-top: 0;
  color: #17356f;
  font-weight: 800;
}

.method-card.direct {
  border-top: 7px solid #2f80ed;
}

.method-card.iterative {
  border-top: 7px solid #20a66a;
}

.method-card ul,
.slide-card ul {
  margin-bottom: 0;
}

.method-card li,
.slide-card li {
  margin: 0.25rem 0;
}

/* Definition environment (Quarto crossref block):
   ::: {#label .definition}
   ## Title
   ...
   ::: */
div.definition.theorem {
  margin: 1.1rem 0;
  padding: 0.95rem 1.15rem 0.95rem 1.25rem;
  border: 1px solid #b8d4f0;
  border-left: 5px solid #0d6efd;
  border-radius: 0.45rem;
  background: #f3f8ff;
}

div.definition.theorem > .theorem-title {
  display: block;
  margin-bottom: 0.55rem;
  color: #0d6efd;
  font-weight: 700;
}
</style>

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## How to use this handout — Evolving Study Notes with an AI Tutor

This handout is the **shared main material for Lectures 5 and 6**.
The companion slides (`5th.html` and `6th.html`) are only for class
logistics and assignment instructions.

Like the previous handouts, this file is designed to grow with you.
Whenever a line confuses you, ask the AI tutor and insert a Q&A block
following [`AI_TUTOR.md`](../AI_TUTOR.md).

For Exercise 5.0 or 6.0, copy this handout into the corresponding
assignment repository and add your own Q&A blocks there.
:::

## Roadmap {.unnumbered}

This handout is split across two lectures:

| Session | Main topics |
|---|---|
| Lecture 5 | linear systems, conditioning, and an introduction to stationary iterative methods (Jacobi and Gauss-Seidel) |
| Lecture 6 | convergence analysis of stationary iterations, symmetric positive definite matrices, conjugate gradient method, and preconditioning |

---

## Systems of Linear Equations {#sec-linear-systems}

::: {.intro-slide}
### Key Message {.unnumbered .unlisted}

<p class="big-message">
Many numerical problems ask us to find <strong>many unknowns at once</strong>.
The simplest and most typical model is a <strong>system of linear equations</strong>.
</p>

<div class="hero-equation">

$$
Ax=b,
$$

</div>

<div class="symbol-strip">
<span class="tag">$A$: coefficient matrix</span>
<span class="tag">$x$: unknown vector</span>
<span class="tag">$b$: given right-hand side</span>
</div>
:::

::: {.intro-slide}
### Example: from equations to matrix form {.unnumbered .unlisted}

$$
\begin{cases}
2x+4y+6z=6,\\
3x+8y+7z=15,\\
5x+7y+21z=24
\end{cases}
$$

This system becomes the compact matrix equation **$Ax=b$**:

$$
A=
\begin{pmatrix}
2&4&6\\
3&8&7\\
5&7&21
\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
b=
\begin{pmatrix}
6\\15\\24
\end{pmatrix}.
$$
:::

::: {.intro-slide}
### Why do linear systems appear everywhere? {.unnumbered .unlisted}

<p class="big-message">
Many complicated numerical problems are solved by repeatedly creating
<strong>linear subproblems</strong>.
</p>

:::: {.slide-grid}
::::: {.slide-card}
#### Nonlinear Equations {.unnumbered .unlisted}
Newton's method repeatedly solves a **linearized system**.
::::

::::: {.slide-card}
#### Optimization {.unnumbered .unlisted}
Local models often lead to **linear algebra subproblems**.
::::

::::: {.slide-card}
#### Differential Equations {.unnumbered .unlisted}
Discretization often produces a **large linear system**.
:::::

::::: {.slide-card}
#### Data and Simulation {.unnumbered .unlisted}
Many parameters must be determined **simultaneously**.
:::::
::::

<p class="big-message">
So <strong>solving $Ax=b$</strong> is a building block for many later methods.
</p>
:::

---

## Direct and Iterative Methods {#sec-direct-iterative}

::: {.intro-slide}
### Two strategies for solving $Ax=b$ {.unnumbered .unlisted}

:::: {.method-grid}
::::: {.method-card .direct}
### Direct Methods {.unnumbered .unlisted}

<p class="big-message">Solve by a finite sequence of algebraic operations.</p>

**Examples:** Gaussian elimination, LU factorization, Cholesky factorization

**Strengths:** stable, widely applicable

**Weaknesses:** large memory, dense cost about **$O(n^3)$**

<div class="tag-row">
<span class="tag">dense matrices</span>
<span class="tag">medium size</span>
</div>
:::::

::::: {.method-card .iterative}
### Iterative Methods {.unnumbered .unlisted}

<p class="big-message">Generate approximations that hopefully converge.</p>

$$
x^{(0)}, x^{(1)}, x^{(2)},\ldots \longrightarrow x
$$

**Examples:** Jacobi, Gauss-Seidel, Krylov methods, CG

**Strengths:** small memory, cheap iterations

**Weaknesses:** convergence is not automatic

<div class="tag-row">
<span class="tag">sparse matrices</span>
<span class="tag">large scale</span>
</div>
:::::
::::
:::

::: {.intro-slide}
### Iterative methods: two families {.unnumbered .unlisted}

:::: {.slide-grid}
::::: {.slide-card}
#### Stationary {.unnumbered .unlisted}

Same update rule at every step.

**Jacobi**, **Gauss-Seidel**
:::::

::::: {.slide-card}
#### Nonstationary {.unnumbered .unlisted}

The update rule changes using information generated during computation.

**Krylov methods**, **Conjugate Gradient (CG)**
:::::
::::

<p class="big-message">
Lecture 5 starts with <strong>stationary methods</strong>; Lecture 6 moves to
<strong>CG and preconditioning</strong>.
</p>
:::

---

## Conditioning of Linear Systems {#sec-conditioning}

Let $A$ be nonsingular. The condition number of $A$ with respect to a
matrix norm is

$$
\operatorname{cond}(A) = \|A\|\,\|A^{-1}\|.
$$

Because $I=AA^{-1}$,

$$
1=\|I\|=\|AA^{-1}\|\leq \|A\|\,\|A^{-1}\|
=\operatorname{cond}(A),
$$

so $\operatorname{cond}(A)\geq 1$.  The value depends on the norm being
used, such as $\ell^1$, $\ell^2$, or $\ell^\infty$.

:::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Definition: singular values"}
For a matrix $A\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times n}$, the **singular values**
$\sigma_1\geq \sigma_2\geq \cdots \geq \sigma_n\geq 0$
are the nonnegative square roots of the eigenvalues of $A^T A$:

$$
\sigma_i=\sqrt{\lambda_i(A^T A)},
\qquad i=1,2,\ldots,n.
$$

Equivalently, they are the diagonal entries of $\Sigma$ in the singular
value decomposition

$$
A=U\Sigma V^T,
$$

where $U,V$ are orthogonal and $\Sigma=\operatorname{diag}(\sigma_1,\ldots,\sigma_n)$.

The largest and smallest singular values are denoted

$$
\sigma_{\max}(A)=\sigma_1,
\qquad
\sigma_{\min}(A)=\sigma_n.
$$

For symmetric positive definite $A$, the singular values coincide with
the eigenvalues.
::::

:::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="Remark: 2-norm condition number"}
For the 2-norm, the condition number can be written using singular
values:

$$
\operatorname{cond}_2(A)
=
\|A\|_2\,\|A^{-1}\|_2
=
\frac{\sigma_{\max}(A)}{\sigma_{\min}(A)},
$$
::::

For the linear system $Ax=b$, suppose $b$ is perturbed to
$b+\Delta b$, and the solution changes from $x$ to $x+\Delta x$:

$$
A(x+\Delta x)=b+\Delta b.
$$

Then

$$
A\Delta x=\Delta b,
\qquad
\Delta x=A^{-1}\Delta b.
$$

Using matrix norms,

$$
\|\Delta x\|
\leq
\|A^{-1}\|\,\|\Delta b\|.
$$

Also, since $b=Ax$,

$$
\|b\|\leq \|A\|\,\|x\|.
$$

Combining these estimates gives

$$
\frac{\|\Delta x\|}{\|x\|}
\leq
\operatorname{cond}(A)
\frac{\|\Delta b\|}{\|b\|}.
$$

A large condition number means that a small relative perturbation in
$b$ can cause a large relative perturbation in the solution.

::: {.callout-note title="What the following code does"}
For each test matrix $A$, we first choose the exact solution

$$
x_* = (1,1,\ldots,1)^T.
$$

Then we define the right-hand side by

$$
b = Ax_*,
$$

so the exact answer is known in advance.  Next, we solve the perturbed
system

$$
A\widehat{x} = b + \Delta b,
\qquad
\frac{\|\Delta b\|}{\|b\|}\approx 10^{-8}.
$$

Finally, we compare the relative perturbation in $b$ with the relative
error in the computed solution:

$$
\texttt{rel\_b}
=
\frac{\|\Delta b\|}{\|b\|},
\qquad
\texttt{rel\_x}
=
\frac{\|\widehat{x}-x_*\|}{\|x_*\|}.
$$

The surprising point is that $\widehat{x}$ can be far from
$(1,1,\ldots,1)^T$ even when $\Delta b$ is tiny.
:::

```{python}
#| eval: false
import numpy as np
from numpy import linalg as LA

def perturbation_demo(A, rng, eps=1e-8):
    n = A.shape[0]

    # Set the exact solution by hand:
    #     x = (1, 1, ..., 1)^T.
    # Then define b from Ax = b, so we know the true answer in advance.
    x_true = np.ones(n)
    b = A @ x_true

    # Add a very small relative perturbation to the right-hand side b.
    # This models the situation where data or rounding errors slightly
    # change the system from Ax = b to Ax = b + db.
    db = eps * LA.norm(b) * rng.normal(size=n)
    x_hat = LA.solve(A, b + db)

    # rel_b measures the relative size of the perturbation in b:
    #     ||db|| / ||b||.
    # rel_x measures the resulting relative error in the solution:
    #     ||x_hat - x_true|| / ||x_true||.
    rel_b = LA.norm(db) / LA.norm(b)
    rel_x = LA.norm(x_hat - x_true) / LA.norm(x_true)
    return LA.cond(A), rel_b, rel_x, x_hat

# These matrices are 10x10 examples prepared for this experiment.
# They have very different condition numbers, so the same small
# perturbation in b produces very different errors in x.
matrices = {
    "A1": np.loadtxt("data/A1.txt", delimiter=","),
    "A2": np.loadtxt("data/A2.txt", delimiter=","),
    "A3": np.loadtxt("data/A3.txt", delimiter=","),
}

rng = np.random.default_rng(2026)
np.set_printoptions(precision=3, suppress=False)

# Display the true solution first.  The surprising point is that
# the computed solution can be far from this vector even though the
# right-hand side perturbation is tiny.
print("true solution x =", np.ones(10))
print()

for name, A in matrices.items():
    cond_A, rel_b, rel_x, x_hat = perturbation_demo(A, rng)
    print(f"{name}: cond(A)={cond_A:10.2e}, "
          f"rel_b={rel_b:10.2e}, "
          f"relative error in x={rel_x:10.2e}")
    print("computed x =", x_hat)
    print()
```

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Show output

```{python}
#| echo: false
import numpy as np
from numpy import linalg as LA

def perturbation_demo(A, rng, eps=1e-8):
    n = A.shape[0]

    # Set the exact solution by hand:
    #     x = (1, 1, ..., 1)^T.
    # Then define b from Ax = b, so we know the true answer in advance.
    x_true = np.ones(n)
    b = A @ x_true

    # Add a very small relative perturbation to the right-hand side b.
    # This models the situation where data or rounding errors slightly
    # change the system from Ax = b to Ax = b + db.
    db = eps * LA.norm(b) * rng.normal(size=n)
    x_hat = LA.solve(A, b + db)

    # rel_b measures the relative size of the perturbation in b:
    #     ||db|| / ||b||.
    # rel_x measures the resulting relative error in the solution:
    #     ||x_hat - x_true|| / ||x_true||.
    rel_b = LA.norm(db) / LA.norm(b)
    rel_x = LA.norm(x_hat - x_true) / LA.norm(x_true)
    return LA.cond(A), rel_b, rel_x, x_hat

# These matrices are 10x10 examples prepared for this experiment.
# They have very different condition numbers, so the same small
# perturbation in b produces very different errors in x.
matrices = {
    "A1": np.loadtxt("data/A1.txt", delimiter=","),
    "A2": np.loadtxt("data/A2.txt", delimiter=","),
    "A3": np.loadtxt("data/A3.txt", delimiter=","),
}

rng = np.random.default_rng(2026)
np.set_printoptions(precision=3, suppress=False)

# Display the true solution first.  The surprising point is that
# the computed solution can be far from this vector even though the
# right-hand side perturbation is tiny.
print("true solution x =", np.ones(10))
print()

for name, A in matrices.items():
    cond_A, rel_b, rel_x, x_hat = perturbation_demo(A, rng)
    print(f"{name}: cond(A)={cond_A:10.2e}, "
          f"rel_b={rel_b:10.2e}, "
          f"relative error in x={rel_x:10.2e}")
    print("computed x =", x_hat)
    print()
```
:::

---

## Stationary Iterative Methods {#sec-stationary-iterative}

We now study two classical **stationary** iterative methods for $Ax=b$:
Jacobi and Gauss-Seidel.
Both start from a matrix splitting $A=D+L+U$ and produce updates of the
form $x^{(k+1)}=g(x^{(k)})$, where $g$ is an affine fixed-point map.

### Matrix Splitting {#sec-splitting}

For stationary iterative methods, write

$$
A = D+L+U,
$$

where

- $D$ is the diagonal part of $A$,
- $L$ is the strictly lower triangular part of $A$,
- $U$ is the strictly upper triangular part of $A$.

Then

$$
Ax=b
$$

becomes

$$
(D+L+U)x=b.
$$

Different rearrangements of this equation lead to different iterative
methods.

In the next two methods, the update will have the form

$$
x^{(k+1)} = g(x^{(k)}),
$$

which is called a **fixed-point iteration**.  Here, $g$ is an affine map
of the form $g(x)=Mx+c$.

### Jacobi Method {#sec-jacobi}

The Jacobi method rearranges

$$
Dx=-(L+U)x+b.
$$

Starting from an initial guess $x^{(0)}$, define

$$
x^{(k+1)}
=
D^{-1}\{b-(L+U)x^{(k)}\}.
$$

Componentwise, this is

$$
x_i^{(k+1)}
=
\frac{1}{a_{ii}}
\left(
b_i-\sum_{j\ne i}a_{ij}x_j^{(k)}
\right).
$$

All components of the new vector are computed from the old vector.

### Gauss-Seidel Method {#sec-gauss-seidel}

The Gauss-Seidel method rearranges

$$
(D+L)x=-Ux+b.
$$

Starting from $x^{(0)}$, define

$$
x^{(k+1)}
=
(D+L)^{-1}(b-Ux^{(k)}).
$$

Componentwise,

$$
x_i^{(k+1)}
=
\frac{1}{a_{ii}}
\left(
b_i
-\sum_{j<i}a_{ij}x_j^{(k+1)}
-\sum_{j>i}a_{ij}x_j^{(k)}
\right).
$$

The newest available values are used immediately.

### Stopping Criterion {#sec-stopping-criterion}

For a small tolerance $\varepsilon>0$, a stationary iteration must be
stopped in finite time.  Two common criteria are based on the size of the
update and the size of the residual.

::: {.callout-note title="Stopping criterion 1: relative update"}
Stop when successive approximations change little:

$$
\frac{\|x^{(k+1)}-x^{(k)}\|}
{\|x^{(k)}\|}
<\varepsilon.
$$

This is easy to compute, but it can sometimes stop before the equation
$Ax=b$ is solved accurately enough.
:::

::: {.callout-note title="Stopping criterion 2: relative residual"}
Stop when the residual is small compared with the right-hand side:

$$
\frac{\|Ax^{(k)}-b\|}
{\|b\|}
<\varepsilon.
$$

This is more reliable, but it costs more because we must compute the
residual $Ax^{(k)}-b$.
:::

### Jacobi Implementation {#sec-jacobi-implementation}

```{python}
#| eval: false
import numpy as np
from numpy import linalg as LA
```

```{python}
#| eval: false
def jacobi(A, b, x0=None, tol=1e-10, max_iter=500):
    A = np.asarray(A, dtype=float)
    b = np.asarray(b, dtype=float)
    n = len(b)
    x = np.zeros(n) if x0 is None else np.asarray(x0, dtype=float).copy()

    D = np.diag(A)
    R = A - np.diagflat(D)
    residuals = []

    for k in range(max_iter):
        x_new = (b - R @ x) / D
        residual = LA.norm(b - A @ x_new)
        residuals.append(residual)

        if residual < tol:
            return x_new, k + 1, np.array(residuals)

        x = x_new

    return x, max_iter, np.array(residuals)

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

A_demo = np.array([
    [10.0, -1.0,  2.0],
    [-2.0, 10.0, -3.0],
    [ 1.0, -5.0, 10.0],
])
x_true = np.ones(3)
b_demo = A_demo @ x_true

x_j, it_j, res_j = jacobi(A_demo, b_demo, tol=1e-10)
print("Jacobi solution :", x_j)
print("iterations      :", it_j)
print("error vs true x :", LA.norm(x_j - x_true))

print("\nConvergence history (selected iterations)")
print(f"{'k':>4} | {'||b - A x_k||':>16}")
print("-" * 25)
sample = sorted(set([0, 1, 2, 5, 10, 20, len(res_j) - 1]))
for k in sample:
    if 0 <= k < len(res_j):
        print(f"{k:4d} | {res_j[k]:16.3e}")

plt.figure(figsize=(6, 4))
plt.semilogy(res_j, marker="o")
plt.xlabel("iteration $k$")
plt.ylabel(r"residual $\|b - A x^{(k)}\|$")
plt.title("Jacobi convergence")
plt.grid(True, which="both", linestyle=":")
plt.show()
```

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Click to reveal the output

```{python}
#| echo: false
import numpy as np
from numpy import linalg as LA
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def jacobi(A, b, x0=None, tol=1e-10, max_iter=500):
    A = np.asarray(A, dtype=float)
    b = np.asarray(b, dtype=float)
    n = len(b)
    x = np.zeros(n) if x0 is None else np.asarray(x0, dtype=float).copy()

    D = np.diag(A)
    R = A - np.diagflat(D)
    residuals = []

    for k in range(max_iter):
        x_new = (b - R @ x) / D
        residual = LA.norm(b - A @ x_new)
        residuals.append(residual)

        if residual < tol:
            return x_new, k + 1, np.array(residuals)

        x = x_new

    return x, max_iter, np.array(residuals)

A_demo = np.array([
    [10.0, -1.0,  2.0],
    [-2.0, 10.0, -3.0],
    [ 1.0, -5.0, 10.0],
])
x_true = np.ones(3)
b_demo = A_demo @ x_true

x_j, it_j, res_j = jacobi(A_demo, b_demo, tol=1e-10)
print("Jacobi solution :", x_j)
print("iterations      :", it_j)
print("error vs true x :", LA.norm(x_j - x_true))

print("\nConvergence history (selected iterations)")
print(f"{'k':>4} | {'||b - A x_k||':>16}")
print("-" * 25)
sample = sorted(set([0, 1, 2, 5, 10, 20, len(res_j) - 1]))
for k in sample:
    if 0 <= k < len(res_j):
        print(f"{k:4d} | {res_j[k]:16.3e}")

plt.figure(figsize=(6, 4))
plt.semilogy(res_j, marker="o")
plt.xlabel("iteration $k$")
plt.ylabel(r"residual $\|b - A x^{(k)}\|$")
plt.title("Jacobi convergence")
plt.grid(True, which="both", linestyle=":")
plt.show()
```
:::

## Convergence Analysis {#sec-fixed-point}

We now use the fixed-point form introduced above to study convergence.
Both Jacobi and Gauss-Seidel can be written as

$$
x^{(k+1)} = Mx^{(k)} + c.
$$

If the sequence converges to $x$, then the limit must satisfy
$x=Mx+c$.

For Jacobi,

$$
M_J=-D^{-1}(L+U),
\qquad
c_J=D^{-1}b.
$$

For Gauss-Seidel,

$$
M_{GS}=-(D+L)^{-1}U,
\qquad
c_{GS}=(D+L)^{-1}b.
$$

The central convergence criterion is stated using the following quantity.

:::: {.callout-note title="Definition: spectral radius"}
The **spectral radius** of a matrix $M$ is defined by

$$
\rho(M)=\max_i |\lambda_i|,
$$

where $\lambda_i$ are the eigenvalues of $M$.
::::

:::: {.callout-important title="Theorem: fixed-point convergence criterion"}
For the fixed-point iteration

$$
x^{(k+1)}=Mx^{(k)}+c
$$

the sequence converges to the unique fixed point for every starting vector
$x^{(0)}$ **if and only if**

$$
\rho(M)<1,
$$

where $\rho(M)$ is the spectral radius of $M$.
::::

:::: {.callout-tip title="Corollary: norm sufficient condition"}
If

$$
\|M\|<1
$$

for some induced matrix norm, then the fixed-point iteration converges.
This follows from

$$
\rho(M)\leq \|M\|.
$$
::::

### Strict Diagonal Dominance {#sec-diagonal-dominance}

:::: {.callout-note title="Definition: strict diagonal dominance"}
A matrix $A=(a_{ij})$ is **strictly diagonally dominant** if

$$
|a_{ii}|
>
\sum_{j\ne i}|a_{ij}|
\qquad
\text{for every } i.
$$

::::

:::: {.callout-important title="Theorem: convergence guarantee"}

If $A$ is strictly diagonally dominant, then both the Jacobi method and
the Gauss-Seidel method converge to the solution of $Ax=b$ for every
initial vector $x^{(0)}$.
::::

For Jacobi, the proof is especially direct:

$$
\|M_J\|_\infty
=
\max_i
\sum_{j\ne i}
\left|\frac{a_{ij}}{a_{ii}}\right|
<
1.
$$

Thus $\rho(M_J)\leq \|M_J\|_\infty<1$.

The Gauss-Seidel proof is a little more involved, but it uses the same
idea: strict diagonal dominance makes the error iteration contract, so
errors are not amplified from one step to the next.

:::: {.callout-note title="Remark: Gauss-Seidel for SPD matrices"}
If $A$ is symmetric positive definite (SPD), then the Gauss-Seidel method
converges for any initial vector $x^{(0)}$. See the beginning of the next
section for the definition of SPD.
::::

:::: {.callout-warning title="Remark: normal equations"}
When $A$ is not symmetric positive definite, one might try to solve the
normal equations

$$
A^T A x = A^T b.
$$

This makes the matrix symmetric and, when $A$ has full column rank, positive
definite. However, it can severely worsen the conditioning:

$$
\operatorname{cond}_2(A^T A)=\operatorname{cond}_2(A)^2.
$$

Therefore, normal equations should be used with care in numerical computations.
::::

---

## Conjugate Gradient Method (CG Method) {#sec-cg}

We now move from **stationary** iterative methods to a **nonstationary**
method designed for symmetric positive definite (SPD) systems.
The **conjugate gradient (CG)** method updates both the approximation
$x^{(k)}$ and the search direction at each step.
By choosing $A$-conjugate directions, CG avoids undoing progress made in
previous iterations, and for SPD problems it usually converges much faster
than Jacobi or Gauss-Seidel.

Krylov subspace iteration methods, including CG, were selected as one of the
**Top 10 Algorithms of the 20th Century**.
([Cipra, 2000](https://archive.siam.org/pdf/news/637.pdf)).
The full citation is Barry A. Cipra, "The Best of the 20th Century:
Editors Name Top 10 Algorithms," *SIAM News*, **33**(4), pp. 1--2, 2000.

### Symmetric Positive Definite Matrices {#sec-spd}

CG applies when the coefficient matrix $A$ is **symmetric positive definite (SPD)**.
The definition of SPD is as follows.

::::: {.callout-note title="Definition: symmetric positive definite"}
A matrix $A\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times n}$ is **symmetric positive definite**
(SPD) if it satisfies

$$
A=A^T
$$

and

$$
x^T A x > 0
\qquad
\text{for every } x\ne 0.
$$
:::::

For SPD matrices, the quadratic function

$$
\phi(x)=\frac12 x^T A x - b^T x
$$

has a unique minimizer, and that minimizer is exactly the solution of $Ax=b$.

Indeed,

$$
\nabla \phi(x)=Ax-b.
$$

Therefore, solving $Ax=b$ is equivalent to minimizing $\phi(x)$.

For SPD $A$, the algorithm is as follows.

:::: {.callout-note title="Algorithm: Conjugate Gradient Method"}
**Input:** SPD matrix $A$, vector $b$, initial guess $x_0$, tolerance
`tol`.

**Output:** approximate solution $x$.

1. Initialize.

$$
r_0=b-Ax_0,
\qquad
p_0=r_0.
$$

2. For $k=0,1,2,\ldots$, repeat the following steps.

   If

   $$
   \|r_k\| < \texttt{tol}.
   $$

   then stop. Otherwise, compute

   $$
   \alpha_k
   =
   \frac{r_k^T r_k}{p_k^T A p_k},
   \qquad
   x_{k+1}
   =
   x_k+\alpha_k p_k,
   $$

   $$
   r_{k+1}
   =
   r_k-\alpha_k A p_k,
   \qquad
   \beta_k
   =
   \frac{r_{k+1}^T r_{k+1}}{r_k^T r_k},
   $$

   $$
   p_{k+1}
   =
   r_{k+1}+\beta_k p_k.
   $$

3. Return the latest approximation $x$.
::::

The Python implementation below follows this algorithm.

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Python implementation of conjugate gradient"}
```{python}
#| eval: false
def conjugate_gradient(A, b, x0=None, tol=1e-10, max_iter=None):
    A = np.asarray(A, dtype=float)
    b = np.asarray(b, dtype=float)
    n = len(b)
    if max_iter is None:
        max_iter = n
    x = np.zeros(n) if x0 is None else np.asarray(x0, dtype=float).copy()

    r = b - A @ x
    p = r.copy()
    residuals = [LA.norm(r)]

    for k in range(max_iter):
        Ap = A @ p
        alpha = (r @ r) / (p @ Ap)
        x = x + alpha * p
        r_new = r - alpha * Ap
        residuals.append(LA.norm(r_new))
        if LA.norm(r_new) < tol:
            return x, k + 1, np.array(residuals)
        beta = (r_new @ r_new) / (r @ r)
        p = r_new + beta * p
        r = r_new

    return x, max_iter, np.array(residuals)

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

n = 20
rng = np.random.default_rng(2026)
Q, _ = LA.qr(rng.normal(size=(n, n)))
eigs = np.linspace(1.0, 100.0, n)
A_cg_demo = Q @ np.diag(eigs) @ Q.T
x_true = np.ones(n)
b_cg_demo = A_cg_demo @ x_true

x_cg, it_cg, res_cg = conjugate_gradient(A_cg_demo, b_cg_demo, tol=1e-10)
print("cond(A)         :", f"{LA.cond(A_cg_demo):.2e}")
print("iterations      :", it_cg)
print("error vs true x :", LA.norm(x_cg - x_true))

print("\nConvergence history (selected iterations)")
print(f"{'k':>4} | {'||b - A x_k||':>16}")
print("-" * 25)
sample = sorted(set([0, 1, 2, 5, 10, 15, len(res_cg) - 1]))
for k in sample:
    if 0 <= k < len(res_cg):
        print(f"{k:4d} | {res_cg[k]:16.3e}")

plt.figure(figsize=(6, 4))
plt.semilogy(res_cg, marker="o")
plt.xlabel("iteration $k$")
plt.ylabel(r"residual $\|b - A x^{(k)}\|$")
plt.title("CG convergence (SPD, $n=20$, $\\kappa_2(A)=100$)")
plt.grid(True, which="both", linestyle=":")
plt.show()
```
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Show output

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def conjugate_gradient(A, b, x0=None, tol=1e-10, max_iter=None):
    A = np.asarray(A, dtype=float)
    b = np.asarray(b, dtype=float)
    n = len(b)
    if max_iter is None:
        max_iter = n
    x = np.zeros(n) if x0 is None else np.asarray(x0, dtype=float).copy()

    r = b - A @ x
    p = r.copy()
    residuals = [LA.norm(r)]

    for k in range(max_iter):
        Ap = A @ p
        alpha = (r @ r) / (p @ Ap)
        x = x + alpha * p
        r_new = r - alpha * Ap
        residuals.append(LA.norm(r_new))
        if LA.norm(r_new) < tol:
            return x, k + 1, np.array(residuals)
        beta = (r_new @ r_new) / (r @ r)
        p = r_new + beta * p
        r = r_new

    return x, max_iter, np.array(residuals)

n = 20
rng = np.random.default_rng(2026)
Q, _ = LA.qr(rng.normal(size=(n, n)))
eigs = np.linspace(1.0, 100.0, n)
A_cg_demo = Q @ np.diag(eigs) @ Q.T
x_true = np.ones(n)
b_cg_demo = A_cg_demo @ x_true

x_cg, it_cg, res_cg = conjugate_gradient(A_cg_demo, b_cg_demo, tol=1e-10)
print("cond(A)         :", f"{LA.cond(A_cg_demo):.2e}")
print("iterations      :", it_cg)
print("error vs true x :", LA.norm(x_cg - x_true))

print("\nConvergence history (selected iterations)")
print(f"{'k':>4} | {'||b - A x_k||':>16}")
print("-" * 25)
sample = sorted(set([0, 1, 2, 5, 10, 15, len(res_cg) - 1]))
for k in sample:
    if 0 <= k < len(res_cg):
        print(f"{k:4d} | {res_cg[k]:16.3e}")

plt.figure(figsize=(6, 4))
plt.semilogy(res_cg, marker="o")
plt.xlabel("iteration $k$")
plt.ylabel(r"residual $\|b - A x^{(k)}\|$")
plt.title("CG convergence (SPD, $n=20$, $\\kappa_2(A)=100$)")
plt.grid(True, which="both", linestyle=":")
plt.show()
```
:::

:::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Optional reading: why CG works"}
For an approximation $x^{(k)}$, the **residual**

$$
r^{(k)}=b-Ax^{(k)}
$$

measures how far the current approximation is from satisfying the equation.
From the quadratic minimization viewpoint, the residual is the negative gradient:

$$
r^{(k)} = -\nabla \phi(x^{(k)}),
$$

Thus, the **steepest descent** method moves in the residual direction.
The CG method improves this idea by choosing search directions that do not
undo progress made by previous directions.

Two vectors $p$ and $q$ are called **$A$-conjugate** if

$$
p^T A q = 0,
$$

This is orthogonality with respect to the inner product
$\langle p,q\rangle_A = p^T A q$.

If $p_0,\ldots,p_{n-1}$ are mutually $A$-conjugate nonzero directions, then
one exact line search along each direction recovers the exact solution in at
most $n$ steps in exact arithmetic. This is the key geometric reason why CG
terminates in finitely many steps for SPD systems.
::::

### CG Convergence and the Condition Number {#sec-cg-convergence}

For SPD matrices, CG has two classical convergence results.
The first says that the method is *finite* in exact arithmetic.
The second gives a quantitative error bound that depends on the condition number.

:::: {.callout-important title="Theorem: finite termination of CG"}
Let $A\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times n}$ be symmetric positive definite.
In exact arithmetic, the conjugate gradient method applied to $Ax=b$
produces the exact solution $x^*=A^{-1}b$ in at most $n$ iterations. That is,

$$
x^{(m)}=x^*
\qquad
\text{for some } m\leq n.
$$
::::

In floating-point arithmetic, this finite termination is generally lost.
The practical convergence rate depends on the condition number

$$
\kappa_2(A)=\|A\|_2\,\|A^{-1}\|_2.
$$

To state a quantitative bound, we use the **$A$-norm**

$$
\|z\|_A = \sqrt{z^T A z},
$$

This is well-defined because $A$ is SPD.

:::: {.callout-important title="Theorem: CG error bound"}
Let $A\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times n}$ be symmetric positive definite, and let
$x^*$ be the exact solution of $Ax=b$. Let $x^{(k)}$ be the $k$-th iterate
of the conjugate gradient method starting from any initial guess $x^{(0)}$.
Then

$$
\frac{\|x^{(k)}-x^*\|_A}{\|x^{(0)}-x^*\|_A}
\leq
2
\left(
\frac{\sqrt{\kappa_2(A)}-1}{\sqrt{\kappa_2(A)}+1}
\right)^k.
$$
::::

The key message is that CG is fast when $A$ is well-conditioned.
A small $\kappa_2(A)$ makes the contraction factor in the bound much smaller
than $1$.

```{python}
#| eval: false
def random_spd(n, condition_number=10, seed=2026):
    rng = np.random.default_rng(seed)
    Q, _ = LA.qr(rng.normal(size=(n, n)))
    eigs = np.linspace(1.0, condition_number, n)
    return Q @ np.diag(eigs) @ Q.T

kappas = [10, 100, 1000]
histories = {}

for kappa in kappas:
    A = random_spd(20, condition_number=kappa, seed=2026)
    x_true = np.ones(20)
    b = A @ x_true
    _, it, res = conjugate_gradient(A, b, tol=1e-8, max_iter=20)
    histories[kappa] = res
    print(f"cond(A) ≈ {LA.cond(A):8.1f}, "
          f"CG iterations = {it:2d}, "
          f"final residual = {res[-1]:.2e}")

plt.figure(figsize=(6, 4))
for kappa, res in histories.items():
    plt.semilogy(res, marker="o", label=fr"$\kappa_2(A)\approx {kappa}$")
plt.xlabel("iteration $k$")
plt.ylabel(r"residual $\|b - A x^{(k)}\|$")
plt.title("CG convergence vs condition number ($n=20$)")
plt.grid(True, which="both", linestyle=":")
plt.legend()
plt.show()
```

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Show output

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
def random_spd(n, condition_number=10, seed=2026):
    rng = np.random.default_rng(seed)
    Q, _ = LA.qr(rng.normal(size=(n, n)))
    eigs = np.linspace(1.0, condition_number, n)
    return Q @ np.diag(eigs) @ Q.T

kappas = [10, 100, 1000]
histories = {}

for kappa in kappas:
    A = random_spd(20, condition_number=kappa, seed=2026)
    x_true = np.ones(20)
    b = A @ x_true
    _, it, res = conjugate_gradient(A, b, tol=1e-8, max_iter=20)
    histories[kappa] = res
    print(f"cond(A) ≈ {LA.cond(A):8.1f}, "
          f"CG iterations = {it:2d}, "
          f"final residual = {res[-1]:.2e}")

plt.figure(figsize=(6, 4))
for kappa, res in histories.items():
    plt.semilogy(res, marker="o", label=fr"$\kappa_2(A)\approx {kappa}$")
plt.xlabel("iteration $k$")
plt.ylabel(r"residual $\|b - A x^{(k)}\|$")
plt.title("CG convergence vs condition number ($n=20$)")
plt.grid(True, which="both", linestyle=":")
plt.legend()
plt.show()
```
:::

---

## Preconditioning {#sec-preconditioning}

Because convergence estimates for CG depend strongly on the condition number,
problems with large condition numbers can converge slowly.
**Preconditioning** is a technique for accelerating CG by using a
**preconditioner** $P$ that is close to $A$ but much cheaper to work with.

Here we consider the case where $A$ is SPD. In the standard preconditioned CG
method, the preconditioner $P$ is also SPD and is chosen so that $Pz=r$ can be
solved cheaply.

::: {.callout-important title="Three quantities to keep in mind"}
| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $x_k$ | approximate solution at the $k$-th iteration |
| $r_k=b-Ax_k$ | **residual** - how far the current $x_k$ is from satisfying the equation |
| $z_k=P^{-1}r_k$ | **preconditioned residual** - the residual "preprocessed" by $P$ |

One iteration has the following flow.

$$
x_k \;\longrightarrow\; r_k=b-Ax_k \;\longrightarrow\; Pz_k=r_k \;\longrightarrow\; z_k \;\longrightarrow\; p_k \;\longrightarrow\; x_{k+1}.
$$

The important point is that the unknown we seek is always **$x$**;
$z$ is not another name for the solution.
The right-hand side of $Pz=r$ is always **the residual $r_k$ at that iteration,
not $b$**.
If $P\approx A$, then $z_k\approx A^{-1}(b-Ax_k)=x^*-x_k$, so $z_k$ can be
interpreted as an approximation to the correction direction toward the true
solution $x^*$.
:::

### Why Do We Need $P$? {#sec-why-preconditioner}

The convergence speed of CG depends strongly on the condition number and the
eigenvalue distribution. The goal of preconditioning is to choose $P$ so that,
instead of solving with $A$ directly,

- equations of the form $Pz=r$ are **cheap to solve**,
- the effective problem seen by CG is **easier to converge on**.

Both properties are needed.

### Form of the Preconditioned CG Method

In the ordinary CG method, the search direction $p_k$ is built from the
residual $r_k$. In preconditioned CG, instead of using $r_k$ directly, we solve

$$
Pz_k=r_k
$$

and use $z_k=P^{-1}r_k$.

:::: {.callout-note title="Algorithm: preconditioned CG"}
**Initialization**

$$
r_0=b-Ax_0,\qquad
Pz_0=r_0,\qquad
p_0=z_0.
$$

**Iteration**

$$
\alpha_k=\frac{r_k^Tz_k}{p_k^TAp_k},
\qquad
x_{k+1}=x_k+\alpha_kp_k,
$$

$$
r_{k+1}=r_k-\alpha_kAp_k,
\qquad
Pz_{k+1}=r_{k+1},
$$

$$
\beta_k=\frac{r_{k+1}^Tz_{k+1}}{r_k^Tz_k},
\qquad
p_{k+1}=z_{k+1}+\beta_kp_k.
$$
::::

### Common Preconditioners

Here we introduce only two representative examples: the Jacobi preconditioner
and the incomplete Cholesky preconditioner.

#### Jacobi Preconditioner

The simplest preconditioner is

$$
P=\operatorname{diag}(A)
$$

In this case, $Pz=r$ can be solved componentwise as

$$
z_i=\frac{r_i}{a_{ii}}
$$

so it is very cheap.

The Jacobi method appeared earlier as an iterative method, but here we use
$P=\operatorname{diag}(A)$ as a **preconditioner**.
The Jacobi preconditioner uses only the diagonal entries of the matrix to
rescale the variables. It is easy to implement, but its effect is limited when
off-diagonal entries are important.

#### Incomplete Cholesky Preconditioner

::: {#def-cholesky .definition}

## Cholesky Factorization

The **Cholesky factorization** of a matrix $A\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times n}$ means
writing $A$ using a lower triangular matrix $L$ whose diagonal entries are all
positive:

$$
A=LL^T
$$

This factorization exists if and only if $A$ is **symmetric positive definite
(SPD)**, and in that case $L$ is unique. See @sec-spd for the definition of SPD.
:::

Once the Cholesky factorization is available, we can solve $Ly=b$ first and
then $L^T x=y$ to obtain the solution $x$ of $Ax=b$. Like Gaussian elimination
and LU factorization, this is a **direct method**.

For large sparse matrices, fill-in and computational cost can be large, so the
complete Cholesky factorization of $A$ itself is often impractical. Therefore,
preconditioning uses a cheap approximation to $A$, as described next.

In **preconditioning**, we use a **cheap approximation to $A$** rather than $A$
itself:

$$
P \approx A
$$

The **incomplete Cholesky (IC)** preconditioner is a typical example:

$$
A \approx \widetilde{L}\widetilde{L}^T =: P
$$

Here, an approximate factorization that keeps only some of the fill-in is used
as $P$. The tradeoff is that $P$ is less accurate than $A$, but $Pz=r$ is much
cheaper to solve. In each iteration,

$$
Pz=r
$$

is solved as

$$
\widetilde{L}\widetilde{L}^Tz=r
$$

using forward and backward substitution.

Compared with the Jacobi preconditioner, it is more expensive to construct and
apply, but when it works well it can greatly improve the condition number and
substantially reduce the number of CG iterations.

:::: {.callout-warning title="Caution: incomplete Cholesky does not always succeed"}
An incomplete Cholesky factorization cannot always be constructed stably.
For some problems, positive pivots can be lost, breaking the positive
definiteness required by PCG. In such cases, stabilized variants such as
shifted incomplete Cholesky are sometimes used.
::::

Another viewpoint is that, when $P=\widetilde{L}\widetilde{L}^T$,

$$
\widetilde{x}=\widetilde{L}^T x,
\qquad
\widetilde{A}=\widetilde{L}^{-1}A\widetilde{L}^{-T},
\qquad
\widetilde{b}=\widetilde{L}^{-1}b
$$

we can also interpret the method as applying ordinary CG to the transformed
system $\widetilde{A}\widetilde{x}=\widetilde{b}$.
Mathematically, this is the same as the preconditioned CG method described
above, where $Pz=r$ is solved at each iteration.
In implementation, it is more efficient to solve
$\widetilde{L}\widetilde{L}^Tz=r$ by triangular substitution, as introduced
above, than to form the transformed matrix $\widetilde{A}$ explicitly.

Practical preconditioners $P$ are problem-dependent. A good $P$ balances the
following three requirements:

- $Pz=r$ is **cheap** to solve,
- the condition number or eigenvalue distribution of the transformed SPD system
  is **substantially improved**,
- the **memory cost** is realistic.


---

## Summary {#sec-summary}

| Topic | Key idea |
|---|---|
| Conditioning | $\operatorname{cond}(A)=\|A\|\|A^{-1}\|$ measures the sensitivity of $Ax=b$ |
| Direct methods | robust, but for dense methods the cost is usually about $O(n^3)$ |
| Iterative methods | generate approximations; useful for large sparse problems |
| Jacobi | uses only old components: $x^{(k+1)}=D^{-1}(b-(L+U)x^{(k)})$ |
| Gauss-Seidel | immediately reuses newly computed components |
| Stationary convergence | $x^{(k+1)}=Mx^{(k)}+c$ converges for all starts if and only if $\rho(M)<1$ |
| Diagonal dominance | a simple sufficient condition for Jacobi and Gauss-Seidel convergence |
| CG | an efficient method for SPD systems, based on $A$-conjugate directions |
| Condition number in CG | smaller $\kappa_2(A)$ usually means faster convergence |
| Preconditioning | improves conditioning while keeping each step cheap by solving $Pz_k=r_k$ with $P\approx A$ |
